ocr.text: In recent analyses of health outcomes, the impact of demographic factors such as sex and age has garnered significant attention.
The accompanying data table presents key metrics that elucidate this relationship, specifically focusing on the area under the
curve (AUC) and its corresponding 95% confidence interval (CI). The AUC is a critical measure used to evaluate the
performance of predictive models, with a value of 0.637 observed in this study.
Model tests, multivariate models on hospitalisation  Area under ROC Curve  CV  AIC  LR test
AUC  (95% CI)  %
Adjusted for sex and age  0
Individual income and education  0.652  (0.647–0.658)  5.2  74,599  372
Relationships with primary care  0.668  (0.663–0.674)  6.2  73,638  1331
Adjusted for sex, age and multimorbidity  0
Individual income and education  0.859  (0.856–0.862)  13.7  56,432  46
Relationships with primary care  0.864  (0.861–0.866)  14.1  55,626  850
Socioeconomy and relationship  0.864  (0.861–0.867)  14.2  55,587  901
This value indicates a moderate level of discriminative ability concerning the outcomes influenced by sex and age
demographics. The 95% CI, ranging from 0.632 to 0.642, further reinforces the precision of the AUC estimate, suggesting that
the model's performance remains consistent within this interval. Additionally, the analysis encompasses a substantial sample
size of 74,959 individuals, enhancing the reliability of the findings. These metrics collectively provide a nuanced understanding
of how demographic variables intersect with health-related metrics, emphasizing the importance of considering sex and age in
predictive modeling and healthcare strategies. As the healthcare landscape continues to evolve, such insights will be crucial in
tailoring interventions and improving patient outcomes across diverse populations.
